steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Boundaries question about joining two independent clauses. Clause 1: “In 2017, the couple converted a vacant lot in the city into an [apiary].” Clause 2: “in the years that followed they acquired nine additional lots and established more than 35 hives.” Both are independent clauses (each has a subject and a complete predicate), so they must be joined with either a semicolon alone, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — apiary,: A comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice — not acceptable in Standard English. ✗
  • Choice B — apiary, and: A comma followed by the coordinating conjunction “and” correctly joins the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice C — apiary and: The coordinating conjunction “and” without a preceding comma is insufficient to join two independent clauses; this creates a run-on. ✗
  • Choice D — apiary: No punctuation or conjunction between two independent clauses is a fused (run-on) sentence. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
